1: Jeduthun — the heading "according to Jeduthun" names a musical guild descended from one of David's three chief Levitical musicians (1 Chr 6:31-47; 16:41-42; 25:1-6). Jeduthun's guild was responsible for portions of the temple music. The heading places this psalm within the institutional liturgy of the Davidic-era worship system, not merely personal devotion. The term dumiyyah (silence/waiting) in the first line is a specialized word for the quiet of expectant waiting rather than mere quietude; the soul is not passive but oriented, straining toward God who has not yet acted.
2: He alone is my rock and my salvation; he is my fortress — the cluster of military and geological metaphors (rock, fortress, salvation) maps directly onto the Judean wilderness geography where David spent years as a fugitive. The limestone crags and fortified outcrops east of Bethlehem and around En-gedi were literal places of refuge. The "only/alone" (ak) refrain runs through the psalm six times, creating its structural spine: YHWH is the only source, making all other resources insufficient by comparison. This absolute exclusive claim is the psalm's theological core.
3: How long will all of you assault a man? — the psalm shifts to address the enemies directly in a manner characteristic of lament psalms. The image of a "leaning wall" and "tottering fence" describes the psalmist's political and social precariousness: not a fortress but a structure about to collapse. In the context of David's reign, this reflects the sustained court opposition he faced — from Saul's partisans, from Absalom's conspiracy, from rival factions within his own court. The enemies treat a single man as though he were a battle formation to be assaulted.
4: With their mouths they bless, but inwardly they curse — the double-speaking of courtiers in honor-shame cultures. ANE court culture required outward deference and praise of the powerful, but the palace intrigue documented throughout David's reign (2 Sam 15-17; 1 Kgs 1) shows that verbal blessing could conceal active plotting. The specific goal — "to bring him down from his position" — is political deposition from social eminence (seto, his height/dignity). The conspiracy is not military but social: the destruction of honor is the weapon.
5: For my hope comes from him — the refrain is repeated (compare v2) but the emotional word changes: from "salvation" (v2) to "hope/expectation" (tiqvati). Tiqvah (hope) carries the sense of expectant waiting, a cord stretched taut toward a future reality. The word is used of Rahab's scarlet cord in Josh 2:18 (the same root: a cord of hope that would preserve her). The psalmist's hope is not optimism but a taut covenant expectation that YHWH will act consistently with his character.
6: I will not be shaken — the refrain returns again but with a subtle strengthening: v2 says "I will not be greatly shaken" (lo rabbah), while v6 says simply "I will not be shaken" (no qualifier). The progressive stripping away of qualification mirrors the psalm's movement from realistic assessment of danger to confident trust. The Hebrew verbal forms here are imperfect — ongoing, unshakeable stability as a present and continuing posture, not a one-time declaration.
7: In God rests my salvation and my honor — the inclusion of kavod (honor/glory) alongside salvation is significant. In honor-shame culture, to be rescued but dishonored is an incomplete rescue. The enemies threatened the psalmist's seto (eminence, v4); YHWH's rescue must therefore include social vindication — the restoration of honor before the community that witnessed the attack. The psalm does not separate spiritual salvation from social standing because ancient Israelite life did not separate them.
8: Pour out your hearts before him — the practice of personal lament as a recognized form of covenant speech. "Pouring out" the heart is the idiom for the unguarded expression of inner distress before YHWH (1 Sam 1:15 — Hannah "poured out her soul" before YHWH). The invitation is to the people collectively, not just individuals: communal lament was a recognized liturgical practice (Joel 1:14; 2:12-17; Lam 2:19). YHWH as "a refuge for us" (maḥseh lanu) is the covenant-guarantee that the lament is heard.
9: Common people are only a breath / people of rank are a deception — the two social categories: benei adam (sons of Adam = common people, the generic human being) and benei ish (sons of man = men of distinction, the aristocratic class). Both are weighed on scales and found lighter than a breath — the scales imagery is from Egypt's Hall of Judgment (weighing of the heart) but used here for YHWH's moral evaluation of all human pretension to power. The wisdom observation is genuinely egalitarian: wealth and rank fail the scale-test equally.
10: Do not trust in extortion; do not take pride in robbery — two specific economic crimes named: ashaq (oppression/extortion, the abuse of economic leverage) and gazal (robbery, violent seizure of property). These are the specific sins condemned in the prophets against exploiting the poor (Amos 4:1; Mic 2:2; Jer 22:13-17). "If wealth increases, do not set your heart on it" — the prohibition against heart-attachment to wealth echoes Deut 8:11-17 and anticipates Matt 6:21.
11: God has spoken once; two things I have heard — the "once... twice" (or X / X+1) numerical formula appears in wisdom poetry (Prov 6:16-19; 30:15-31; Job 33:14; Amos 1-2) as a rhetorical device emphasizing completeness and authority. The double hearing from a single divine speech underscores the certainty of what follows. In prophetic tradition, YHWH's word spoken once was sufficient and certain (Isa 55:10-11); the psalmist's "twice" represents the hearer's complete reception, not a deficiency in the divine utterance.
12: Power belongs to God / and to you, O Lord, belongs steadfast love — the pairing of oz (power/strength) and hesed (steadfast love/covenant loyalty) as the two divine attributes is the same pairing as in Ps 59:16-17. Power without love produces tyranny; love without power produces ineffectiveness. YHWH's governance of history combines both: the power to enforce and the loyalty to keep covenant. "For you repay each person according to their deeds" — the covenant guarantee that moral order is underwritten by the one who has both power and loyalty.
